definition: A loss function L(ŷ, y) measures how wrong a model's prediction ŷ is compared to the true target y on a single labeled example. Training minimizes the average loss over the dataset to learn parameters θ. This topic is the canonical home for MSE, MAE, binary cross-entropy (BCE), and categorical cross-entropy (CE) in supervised ML.
layman: A loss function is the training scorecard: it tells the model how badly it missed the correct answer on each example. Big misses get a big penalty; small misses get a small one. Pick the right scorecard for the job — squared error for typical regression, absolute error when outliers matter, cross-entropy when predicting class probabilities.
content:
## Loss functions in supervised learning

In **supervised learning**, each training example is **(x, y)** and the model outputs **ŷ = f_θ(x)**. A **loss function** **L(ŷ, y)** scores one prediction. **Training** adjusts **θ** to minimize the **empirical risk** (average loss):

**L̄(θ) = (1/n) Σᵢ L(ŷᵢ, yᵢ)**

This topic is the **canonical home** for the four workhorse losses in classical supervised ML: **MSE**, **MAE**, **BCE**, and **CE**. Problem-type choice (regression vs classification) is covered in **Regression vs Classification**; here we focus on **what each loss measures** and **when to use it**.

### Regression losses

When **y** is continuous:

| Loss | Formula (one example) | Typical use |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | **(y − ŷ)²** | Default regression; penalizes large errors heavily |
| **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error) | **\|y − ŷ\|** | Robust to outliers; equal weight per unit error |

**Batch MSE:** **(1/n) Σ (yᵢ − ŷᵢ)²**. **Batch MAE:** **(1/n) Σ \|yᵢ − ŷᵢ\|**.

**MSE** is differentiable everywhere and convex in **ŷ** for fixed **y**. Squaring makes a prediction that is off by 2 count four times more than one off by 1 — useful when large errors are especially costly.

**MAE** grows linearly with error magnitude. A single wild outlier contributes less relative weight than under MSE. **RMSE** and **MAE as evaluation metrics** (on held-out data) are detailed in **RMSE & MAE**; the same formulas often appear as training loss and as test metrics, but the roles differ.

**MLE link:** Minimizing MSE on Gaussian noise targets is equivalent to **maximum likelihood** for the conditional mean under homoscedastic Gaussian errors (see **Maximum Likelihood Estimation**).

### Classification losses

When **y** is a class label, losses compare the model's **predicted distribution Q** to the **true distribution P** (usually one-hot on the correct class).

The information-theoretic definition **H(P, Q) = −Σ p(x) log q(x)** lives in **Cross-entropy** — including the Shannon derivation and the identity **H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P || Q)**. This topic **uses** that definition without repeating it.

From **Cross-entropy**, the supervised ML forms are:

| Loss | Setting | Per-example formula |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **BCE** (Binary Cross-Entropy) | Binary / Bernoulli | **−y log ŷ − (1−y) log(1−ŷ)** with **ŷ = P(y=1)** |
| **CE** (Categorical Cross-Entropy) | Multi-class, one label | **−log ŷ_y** where **ŷ** is softmax over classes |

**BCE** is **H(P, Q)** when **P** and **Q** are Bernoulli distributions. **CE** for one-hot **P** reduces to **−log q(y_true)** — the standard softmax loss.

**MLE link:** Minimizing average BCE or CE equals maximizing **Bernoulli / Categorical log-likelihood** (see **Maximum Likelihood Estimation**).

### Choosing a loss

```
continuous y  →  MSE (default) or MAE (outlier-heavy)
binary y      →  BCE with sigmoid ŷ
multi-class y →  CE with softmax ŷ (mutually exclusive labels)
```

Do **not** use MSE on one-hot class vectors for classification — it does not match the Bernoulli/Categorical likelihood and gives weak gradients. Use **BCE** or **CE** instead.

### Loss vs metric

| Role | When | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Training loss** | Optimized by gradient descent during fit | MSE, MAE, BCE, CE |
| **Evaluation metric** | Reported on validation/test; may differ from training loss | RMSE, MAE, accuracy, log loss |

You may train with MSE but report RMSE on test data (RMSE = √MSE). Classification accuracy is not differentiable and is rarely the direct training objective.

### Worked regression example

True **y = 10**, prediction **ŷ = 7**:

- **MSE** = (10 − 7)² = **9**
- **MAE** = \|10 − 7\| = **3**

If a second example has **y = 10**, **ŷ = 20** (large miss):

- Its MSE = 100 vs 9 for the first — dominates the batch average.
- Its MAE = 10 vs 3 — still largest, but less explosively so.

### Worked classification example

True class index **y = 2**, softmax **ŷ = [0.1, 0.2, 0.7]**:

- **CE** = **−log(0.7) ≈ 0.36** nats.

If **ŷ = [0.6, 0.3, 0.1]** on the same label:

- **CE** = **−log(0.1) ≈ 2.3** nats — confident wrong prediction is heavily penalized (see **Cross-entropy** for why).

### Summary map

- **MSE / MAE** — regression targets; squared vs absolute penalty.
- **BCE** — binary labels with sigmoid probability.
- **CE** — single-label multi-class with softmax probabilities.
- **H(P, Q) theory** — **Cross-entropy** topic (link, do not duplicate Shannon derivation here).
- **MLE equivalence** — **Maximum Likelihood Estimation** topic.
- **Metrics on held-out data** — **RMSE & MAE** topic.
keyPoints: Loss L(ŷ, y) scores one prediction; training minimizes average loss over labeled data.
MSE = (y − ŷ)² and MAE = |y − ŷ| are the standard regression losses; MSE penalizes large errors more.
BCE and CE are supervised classification losses derived from H(P, Q) — see Cross-entropy for the canonical H(P, Q) definition.
One-hot multi-class CE reduces to −log ŷ_y; BCE handles binary sigmoid outputs.
Minimizing BCE/CE equals MLE under Bernoulli/Categorical models; MSE relates to Gaussian MLE for regression.
